summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while he was previously skeptical about 64-bit desktop operating systems due to compatibility issues, several converging factors suggest a broad transition is imminent. Cheap memory prices making 4GB configurations common, the inability of 32-bit systems to address more than ~3GB, and video card memory demands all push toward 64-bit adoption. He notes that 64-bit won't provide significant performance gains for typical desktop use, but it's necessary to fully utilize modern hardware. After upgrading to Vista x64 himself, he found the experience surprisingly smooth with no driver or compatibility problems. The 64-bit transition may be the last bit transition in our lifetimes, as 64-bit can address 2 exabytes—a four-billion-fold increase over 32-bit.
key_points: Memory has become so cheap that 4GB configurations are mainstream, but 32-bit systems can only address about 3GB, leaving users unable to use what they paid for
Modern video cards with 256-512MB of onboard memory further reduce the usable address space in 32-bit systems
The 64-bit transition increases address space by a factor of four billion, potentially making this the last bit transition in our lifetimes
64-bit offers no significant performance advantage for typical desktop applications—you upgrade for memory access, not speed
Real-world experience with Vista x64 proved surprisingly smooth, with all hardware and software working without issues
money_quotes: Good luck explaining to the average user why their computer says they only have 3 GB of memory, even though they paid for 4.
The switch to 64-bit is interesting because there's a certain air of finality to it. It may be the last bit transition in our lifetimes.
The transition from 32 to 64 bit, whenever we finally make it, will increase our address space by a factor of four billion.
You certainly won't be upgrading to 64-bit applications for better performance. Or at least you shouldn't be, unless you enjoy disappointment.
Unless you have more than 2 GB of memory, there's no reason to bother with 64-bit.
Hopefully we can stay with 64-bit for the foreseeable future, and leave that pesky 128-bit problem for our kids to deal with.
key_insight: The 64-bit desktop transition is driven not by performance gains but by the simple economic reality that memory has become too cheap to be artificially constrained by 32-bit addressing limits.
